coredata.dc:title: Sand bath assisted green synthesis of carbon dots from citrus fruit peels for free radical scavenging and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Gudimella, Krishna Kanthi
Appidi, Tejaswini
Wu, Hui-Fen
Battula, Venkateswararao
Jogdand, Anil
Rengan, Aravind Kumar
Gedda, Gangaraju
coredata.dc:description: A sand bath assisted strategy for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using citrus fruit peels as a renewable green resource is reported in this work. The CDs were synthesized without any alkaline or acidic environment. The synthesized CDs were characterized by various spectroscopic and microscopy techniques. The CDs exhibited excellent water solubility and superior optical properties like excitation dependent emission, and multicolor fluorescence. In addition, the CDs have exhibited remarkable free radical scavenging activity (EC50: 4.7382 μg ml−1).The CDs were highly biocompatible and showed lower toxicity. The CDs when modified with folic acid have shown a significant potential as biological labels for cellular imaging at multiple excitations. Synthesis of CDs from natural fruit peels as an excellent carbon source for versatile applications has been demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Citrus fruit
Peel
Cell imaging
Sand-bath
Free radical scavenging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of FA-CDs (CDs conjugated with Folic acid (FA))
Detection of free radical scavenging activity
Bioimaging of CDs in MCF-7 cells
Structural, morphological and optical characterization of the CDs
Probing the free radical scavenging abilityof CDs
The CDs as effective probes for biocompatibility and multicolor imaging experiments

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors duly acknowledge Dept. of Science & Technology (DST), Govt. of India for research funding (DST/INSPIRE/04/2015/000377). All the authors acknowledge GITAM University Hyderabad & Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad, Telangana, India for providing the resources and infrastructure for the completion of this study. Tejaswini Appidi acknowledges Dept. of Science & Technology, Govt. of India for DST- Inspire PhD fellowship (IF160291).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of sand bath assisted synthesis of CDs.
UV spectra and TEM images. A. Absorbance spectra, B. Fluorescence spectra of CD (Inset shows the images of CD under bright light and UV light), C. HRTEM imaging of CDs.
Fluorescence spectra. A. Fluorescence spectra showing excitation dependent emission of CDs, B. Normalized emission spectra.
DPPH assay. A. UV Absorption spectra of CDs (various concentrations 2 to 20 μL).
B. Inhibition of DPPH by CDs and Ascorbic acid, C. Resultant color change DPPH.
Biocompatibility of CDs in L929 cells.
Bio imaging of CD & CD FA in cancer cells (MCF-7).
Comparison of EC50 values of CDs derived from various precursors.

head.ce:title: Sand bath assisted green synthesis of carbon dots from citrus fruit peels for free radical scavenging and cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sand bath assisted strategy for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using citrus fruit peels as a renewable green resource is reported in this work. The CDs were synthesized without any alkaline or acidic environment. The synthesized CDs were characterized by various spectroscopic and microscopy techniques. The CDs exhibited excellent water solubility and superior optical properties like excitation dependent emission, and multicolor fluorescence. In addition, the CDs have exhibited remarkable free radical scavenging activity (EC50: 4.7382 μg ml−1).The CDs were highly biocompatible and showed lower toxicity. The CDs when modified with folic acid have shown a significant potential as biological labels for cellular imaging at multiple excitations. Synthesis of CDs from natural fruit peels as an excellent carbon source for versatile applications has been demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent advances in nanoscience and nanotechnology have opened new platforms for the applications of nanomaterials in different fields [1]. The spotting of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) in 2004 has provided a lot of scope in carbon material’s research and development due to numerous unique properties like remarkable solubility (in aqueous solution and organic solvents), photoluminescence, excellent photo-stability, multicolor fluorescence emission, and high fluorescence with high quantum yield [2–6]. Also, these materials exhibitlow toxicity and high biocompatibility [7]. These outstanding features in CDs have marked them as a promising material for various applications such as optical devices, fluorescent markers, bio-imaging, drug delivery, sensing, and medical diagnosis [8–12]. Besides, recently, the free radical scavenging ability of CDshas been reported [13]. Thus, these carbon materials are superior to the toxic and expensive semiconductor quantum dots and metal nanomaterials [14]. Therefore, it is essential to explore a novel method for the large-scale synthesis of CDs.

Laser ablation, thermal treatment, arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, microwave treatment, ultra-sonication, and hydrothermal treatment have been widely used for the synthesis of CDs [15–17]. Further, significant effort has been devoted to developing green synthetic methods for synthesis of CDs using inexpensive and renewable natural resources like bio-waste as precursor materials including prawn shells, crab shells, green tea waste, human hairs, cow manure, kitchen waste, etc [18–26]. However, most of these strategies use either strong acids/bases or expensive instruments, and requires further surface-passivation [3]. In general, a sand bath is used to supply uniform heat energy during chemical reaction [27]. This method is also used as a heat source for treatment of biomass [28]. Therefore it can be an alternative to conventional methods for the synthesis of CDs [6,29]. Nevertheless, most of the CDs showed either blue or green emission. Therefore, developing a strategy for the production of multicolor fluorescent CDs without using chemicals or expensive instruments in a simple, economical, environmentally friendly, and green approach is of great interest.

Presently, a lot of domestic waste including citruspeels were discardedinto the environment due to lack of knowledge and non-availability to convert them as useful precursors [30,31]. The usage of solid waste should be properly monitored to reduce waste and in turn, avoid environmental pollution. Chemically, allpeel wastes constitutecarbon in the form of polysaccharides and bio-molecules [19]. Therefore, an accurate, efficient and simple solution to resolve this issue is to convert biowastes into precursors for the production of CDs instead of dumping [32] them is proposed in the present work.

In this study, we established the design of advanced ‘green solution’ for the disposal of domestic waste such as citrus fruit peels, by the production of CDs via a unique sand bath method. This method is an eco-friendly, economically viable, facile, and green approach for the large-scale synthesis of CDs. The synthesis process does not involve any strong acid/base, organic solvent, or surface passivation. Our results revealed that the prepared CDs exhibited excellent photoluminescence properties, multicolor fluorescence, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and low cost. Furthermore, these CDs exhibited great potential for excellent free radical scavenging and (in vitro) cell bio-imaging.

1,1-Diphenyl-2trinitrophenylhydrazine(DPPH), Ethanol, Dimethyl sulfoxide(DMSO) were purchased from SRL Chemicals, India. 1-ethyl-3- [3-(dimethylamino)propyl]carbodiimide (EDC, 99 %) was procured from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA).Trypsin-EDTA, DMEM, RPMI, Calf Serum (US origin), Phosphate buffer at pH 7.0 were purchased from Hi-media Chemicals, (Mumbai, India). 4′,6-Diamidine-2′-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI) was purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, 97.5 %), Folic acid, N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (sulfo-NHS, 97 %), were procured from SRL Chemicals, India. All chemicals were of reagent grade and used as received. Ultra-pure deionized water (Milli Q) was used for the aqueous solution preparations in all experiments.The fluorescence of the samples was observed under a UV lamp (365 nm, Analytik Jena, CA, U.S.A). CDs & FA-CDs were characterized by a UV–visible (UV 1800, Shimadzu, Japan), Fluorescence (RF 6000, Shimadzu, Japan) and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) (Bruker ATR-FTIR, U.S.A) spectroscopy. The zeta potential was evaluated using particle size analyzer (Nicomp 3000, U.S.A). The shape and morphology were studied by a Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100, 200 kV, U.S.A). Bioimaging was performed using fluorescent microscopy (Olympus CKX-53, U.S.A). ImageJ software was used for calculating the diameter and d-spacing of CDs of TEM.

Typically, 2 g of the citrus peel powder was dissolved into 60 ml of deionized water in a round bottom flask. The obtained orange color solution was placed on a sand bath at 180°C under magnetic stirring. The reaction was continued for 12 h until the color of the solution turned to brown. The resulting CDs solutions were further purified by centrifugation, followed by a syringe filtration (0.22 μm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of FA-CDs (CDs conjugated with Folic acid (FA))
ce:section.ce:para: Conjugation of CDs with Folic Acid (FA) was performed using the EDC-NHS coupling [33]. EDC (15 mg) and sulfo-NHS (25 mg) in 1 mL of 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) buffer solutions were added to 2 mL of CDs solution (15 mg/mL) and then stirred in dark at room temperature for 40 min. Subsequently, 1 mL of MES solution containing folic acid (1.25 mg) was added to the activated CDs solution, and the mixture was stirred gently at room temperature in the dark. After 24 h, the reaction mixture was dialyzed for about 6 h to remove the unreacted materials, and the final product was obtained. [10,34]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of free radical scavenging activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Varying volumes (2−20 μL) of CDs were taken and each volume was made up to 40 μL using DMSO as the solvent. Then 3.0 mL of 0.1 mM DPPH (in ethanol) was added and incubated in dark for 30 min. DPPH (3.0 mL) was used as a positive control. Ascorbic acid was used as a reference. The absorbance of each sample mixture was measured at 517 nm. The percentage of Radical Scavenging Activity (% RSA) was calculated using the below formula:
% RSA =
Absorbance control
DPPH
− Absorbance sample
CDs + DPPH
Absorbance control
× 100

Human breast carcinoma (MCF-7), Mice fibroblasts (L929) were obtained from the National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India. The cell lines were cultured in DMEM/RPMI medium supplemented with 10 % (v/v) Fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1 % l-Glutamine, and 100 U/mL penicillin/streptomycin and were maintained at 37 °C in a humid atmosphere containing 5% CO2 under sterile conditions. [35]

In-vitro biocompatibility studies were performed in the L929 cell lines. The viability assay was performed in triplicates. Briefly, cells were seeded in a 96 well plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well for 24 h prior tothe experiments. Next day, the cells were treated with CDs diluted in media at various concentrations for investigation of biocompatibility/cytotoxicity. Untreated cells were considered as controls. 24 h after the treatment, the cells were washed and cell viability was calculated by the MTT assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging of CDs in MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The inherent fluorescence of CDs & FA-CDs was used to investigate the bioimaging for the MCF-7 cells. The cells were seeded in 96 well plates at an estimated density of 1 × 104 cells/well. Next day, fresh media containing, CDs & FA-CDs were incubated with cells for 24 h. Cells without any treatment were considered as controls. For microscopic analysis, the cells were rinsed with PBS and cells were observed under a fluorescent microscope, subjected to UV light (330−385 nm), blue light (450−480 nm) and green light (510−550 nm) excitations. The respective fluorescence corresponding to various excitations was observed and the images were captured using a fluorescent microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, morphological and optical characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Citrus fruit peels, a renewable natural resource that contains cellulose, proteins, flavonoids, and natural phenols [36,37]. Hence, the citrus fruit peels act as remarkable green and natural precursors for the synthesis and preparation of CDs without the use of any chemicals. In addition to the traditionally synthetic methods for CDs like hydrothermal and microwave synthesis, an alternate method using a sand bath was developed to synthesize the CDs in the current study. The synthesis method was illustrated in Fig. 1 . Uniformly sieved sand was used to maintain a constant temperature throughout the process of the formation of CDs from the citrus fruit peels powder solution. Typically, the cellulose in the citrus peels undergoes hydrolysis at 180°C [38–40]. Simultaneously, phenols undergo degradation at or above 180 °C. Also, proteins and flavonoids are thermally unstable at higher temperatures. Thus, these bio-molecules were altered into CDs probably via dehydration and carbonization [16]. One of the interesting features of this synthesis technique is that it neither involves any strong acid or base for the initiation of carbonization nor any surface passivating agent. The yield obtained was 60 mg/mL.

The optical properties of CDs were characterized by UV-visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs appeared yellow upon dilution (1:10) in daylight and bright blue color under UV light (365 nm), respectively (Inset figures in Fig. 2 Aand 2B). The absorption spectra of CDs showed peaks at 280 and 350 nm corresponding to (π→π*) transition of CC at the aromatic region and (n→π*) transition of surface state region, respectively. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to study themorphology of CDs (Fig. 2C, and Fig. S1, supporting materials). The TEM image shows a group of CDs with well-resolved lattice fringes. The arrows point to the CDs and their lattice fringes. At a magnification of 800,000x, we could clearly observe the independent CDs (arrows pointing to the CDs). An average diameter of 4.6 ± 0.28nm with lattice spacing of 0.2 nm was recorded using ImageJ.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 520nm are shown in Fig. 3 A and B. The maximum emission peak at 510 nm was observed for an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. From these results, we can conclude that the PL intensity of CDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength. The emission behavior of CDs under fluorescence microscopy at different excitation wavelengths using a UV light (330−385 nm), blue light (450−480 nm), and green light (510−550 nm) are shown in Fig. 4 .The results demonstrated that the prepared CDs exhibited multiple colors at different excitation wavelengths, which allows them to act as effective fluorescent probes for cell imaging studies. Fourier transformation infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and zeta potential were used in probing the composition of the surface functional groups and charge of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S2A, the FTIR spectrum showed a strong peak above 3400 cm−1 indicating the presence of COOH and OH. Moreover, the sharp peak at 1760 cm−1corresponds to CO. The zeta potential of CDs was found to be-8.78 ± 0.670 mV (Fig. S2B) indicating that the CDs possess negative functional groups like −COOH and−OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing the free radical scavenging abilityof CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the application of CDs, free radical scavenging ability of the synthesized CDs was determined using a slightly modified DPPH assay [41]. Even though many of the nanomaterials are known to afford oxidative stress to the bio-system [42], CDs were considered exceptional owing to their non-toxicity and cost-effectiveness. DPPH assay is a widely employed method to estimate the free radical scavenging ability ofmaterial due to its rapidity and simplicity. DPPH is a long-lasting, nitrogen-containing free radical and appears deep purple. It turns yellow upon reaction with an antioxidant. To estimate the free radical scavenging ability of CDs, DPPH was added to various concentrations of 50−500 μg/mL of CDs and were analysed by the UV–vis spectroscopy at 517 nm.As shown in Fig. 5 A, the intensity of absorbance at 517 nm showed a decrease with the increase in the concentration of CDs. Growth in free radical scavenging capacity (Table S1) was observed from 66 % to 79 % with the increase in concentrations (Fig. 5B). As displayed in the image Fig. 5C, a gradual color change from purple to yellow was observed in treating DPPH with CDs. This was probably due to the electron-donating negative functional groupson the surface of CDs. The estimated EC50 [43] (amount required to exhibit 50 % of the activity) value of CDs and ascorbic acid was evaluated as 4.73829 and 0.80 μg ml−1 (Fig. S3, supporting materials), respectively using Origin 9 software. The EC50 value of CDs is much lower when compared to the reported CDs indicated in Table1 [44–46]. These results demonstrated that synthesized CDs are remarkable materials for free radical scavenging activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CDs as effective probes for biocompatibility and multicolor imaging experiments

ce:section.ce:para: We have successfully developed a sand bath heat-assisted method for the synthesis of CDs using citrus peel waste as a renewable green natural source. This synthesis strategy doesn’t involve complex and extreme synthesis conditions and expensive precursor materials. The-synthesized CDs showed excellent optical properties like excitation dependent emission and were biocompatible. The CDs showed significant free radical scavenging activity (EC50: 4.7382 μg ml-1). The CDs and folic acid-modified CDs (FA-CDs) exhibited photostability with no considerable loss in intensity for 48 h. FA-CDs facilitated in active targeting to cancer cells resulting in enhanced cellular uptake, with a bright fluorescent emission at multiple excitations.

Krishna Kanthi Gudimella: Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Tejaswini Appidi: Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Hui-Fen Wu: Formal analysis. Venkateswararao Battula: Formal analysis. Anil Jogdand: Data curation. Aravind Kumar Rengan: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Gangaraju Gedda: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest regarding the publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors duly acknowledge Dept. of Science & Technology (DST), Govt. of India for research funding (DST/INSPIRE/04/2015/000377). All the authors acknowledge GITAM University Hyderabad & Indian Institute of Technology Hyderabad, Telangana, India for providing the resources and infrastructure for the completion of this study. Tejaswini Appidi acknowledges Dept. of Science & Technology, Govt. of India for DST- Inspire PhD fellowship (IF160291).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2020.111362.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
